covered_sections: 4.1 What is a cell cycle?>Cell division

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: In a cell cycle where interphase occupies 360° of a 360° circle (if drawn as a pie chart) but in reality interphase takes 22 hours of a 24-hour cycle, what percentage of the cycle does interphase represent?
questions.hint: Divide interphase hours by total hours, multiply by 100.
questions.explanation: Percentage = (22 hours / 24 hours) × 100 = 91.67%. Option A (75%) would be 18 hours; C (50%) would be 12 hours.

explanations_tiered.definition: Interphase occupies most of the cell cycle time, typically 90-95%.
explanations_tiered.beginner: If you sleep 22 hours out of 24, you're asleep about 92% of the day — same for interphase.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: 22 hours out of 24 = 22/24 = 0.9167, multiplied by 100 gives 91.7%.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Cell cycle timing experiments using labeled thymidine show interphase duration varies but generally represents 90-95% of cycle time in rapidly dividing cells.
worked_solution: Step 2: Identify total cycle duration = 24 hours.
reasoning_steps: Divide interphase time by total time.
Multiply by 100 to get percentage.
Round to one decimal.

glossary_entry.definition: Longest phase of the cell cycle, occupying about 90-95% of total cycle time.

flashcard.front: What percentage of a 24-hour cell cycle is interphase (22 hours)?

explanations_tiered.solution_manual_note: Answer B. (22/24)×100 = 91.7%; 75% would be 18 hours, not 22.

publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Longest cell cycle stage, comprising G1, S, and G2 phases.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Use circle graphs to visualize interphase dominance.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Outline the series of events taking place in the cell cycle

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: During crossing over in prophase I, a segment of a maternal chromatid is exchanged with a segment of a paternal chromatid. What is the direct consequence of this exchange?

options.A: The chromosome number is reduced from diploid to haploid
options.B: New combinations of alleles are formed on the same chromosome
options.D: The cell immediately enters anaphase I

questions.hint: Think about allele shuffling on a single chromosome.
questions.explanation: Crossing over creates recombinant chromosomes with new allele combinations, increasing genetic diversity. Option A is reduction division (anaphase I), not crossing over.

source_trace.content_anchor: crossing over occurs between non-sister chromatids

explanations_tiered.definition: Crossing over exchanges DNA segments between non-sister chromatids, creating recombinant chromosomes.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: This process shuffles alleles, producing gametes with novel genetic combinations.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Swapping patches between two differently colored strings creates new color patterns.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Crossing over results in new haplotypes: maternal and paternal alleles become mixed on the same chromosome.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Homologous recombination at chiasmata generates new linkage combinations, breaking linkage disequilibrium and increasing genetic variation.
reasoning_steps: Recall that crossing over exchanges DNA segments.
Note that maternal and paternal chromatids have different alleles.
Exchange creates chromosomes with mixed alleles.
misconceptions: Some think crossing over changes ploidy; it does not.
It does not cause immediate anaphase.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Crossing over = new allele combinations on same chromosome.

glossary_entry.definition: Exchange of genetic material between non-sister chromatids, producing recombinant chromosomes.

flashcard.front: What is the genetic result of crossing over?
flashcard.back: New combinations of alleles on the same chromosome.

explanations_tiered.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Crossing over forms new allele combinations; ploidy reduction occurs in anaphase I.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Recombinant chromosomes with new allele combinations.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Exchange of DNA between non-sister chromatids, generating new allele combinations.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Use two-color bead models to show mixing of colors.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Outline the pattern of cell division by meiosis

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: A student believes that chromosomes become visible in prophase because the nuclear envelope breaks down. Why is this belief incomplete?

options.A: Chromosomes become visible primarily due to chromatin condensation, not envelope breakdown
options.B: The nuclear envelope breaks down after chromosomes are already visible
options.C: Chromosomes are always visible, regardless of envelope status
options.D: The nucleolus causes visibility, not the envelope

questions.hint: Think about what makes chromatin thicken.
questions.explanation: Chromosomes become visible because chromatin condenses via condensin complexes; envelope breakdown is separate. Option B is incorrect; condensation and breakdown occur concurrently, but condensation is the primary cause.

source_trace.content_anchor: chromosomes shorten, thicken and become visible

explanations_tiered.definition: Chromosome visibility is due to chromatin condensation, not solely nuclear envelope breakdown.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Condensin complexes coil chromatin 10,000-fold, making chromosomes thick enough to scatter light.
explanations_tiered.beginner: A thin string is hard to see; coiling it into a thick rope makes it visible — condensation, not just uncovering.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Even without a nuclear envelope, uncondensed chromatin remains diffuse and invisible; condensation is essential for visibility.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Condensin I and II introduce positive supercoils, compacting chromatin; envelope breakdown merely removes a barrier but does not alter compaction state.
reasoning_steps: Recall that chromatin is normally diffuse.
Visibility requires condensation, not just access.
Nuclear envelope breakdown removes barrier but does not condense DNA.
misconceptions: Some think visibility = exposure; condensation is key.
Envelope breakdown and condensation occur concurrently but are independent.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Visibility = condensation, not just envelope breakdown.

glossary_entry.definition: Coiling and compaction of chromatin into visible chromosomes during prophase.

flashcard.front: Why do chromosomes become visible in prophase?
flashcard.back: Primarily due to chromatin condensation, not just nuclear envelope breakdown.

explanations_tiered.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Condensation makes chromosomes visible; envelope breakdown alone would not suffice.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What truly makes chromosomes visible in prophase?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Chromatin condensation, not just nuclear envelope breakdown.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Coiling of chromatin into visible chromosomes during prophase.
publishing_metadata.revision_note: Prophase visibility = condensation, not just exposure.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Explain that uncondensed chromatin is invisible even without a nucleus.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Outline the pattern of mitotic cell division

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: A researcher observes that all chromosomes are aligned at the equatorial plate, but one chromosome has both kinetochores attached to spindle fibers from the same pole. How would you classify this cell?
questions.explanation: Although aligned, improper attachment (syntelic) means the cell has not satisfied the spindle assembly checkpoint and is still in prometaphase. Option A is incorrect because normal metaphase requires bipolar attachment.

source_trace.content_anchor: each sister chromatids are attached to a spindle fibre originating from opposite poles

explanations_tiered.definition: Metaphase requires chromosomes aligned at equator with bipolar (amphitelic) attachment to spindle fibers.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Syntelic attachment (both from same pole) prevents anaphase onset due to spindle checkpoint activation.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Even if everyone is lined up, if one person has both ropes attached to the same side, the race (anaphase) cannot start.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: The spindle assembly checkpoint (SAC) detects unattached or improperly attached kinetochores and arrests the cell in prometaphase.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Mad2 and BubR1 at unattached kinetochores inhibit APC/C, preventing securin degradation and anaphase entry until all attachments are amphitelic.
reasoning_steps: Recall that true metaphase requires bipolar attachment.
Same-pole attachment is an error.
Cell will arrest in prometaphase until corrected.
misconceptions: Some think alignment alone defines metaphase; attachment state matters.
Error correction occurs before anaphase, not after.

glossary_entry.definition: Improper attachment where both sister kinetochores attach to same pole, activating the spindle checkpoint.

flashcard.front: Alignment with same-pole spindle attachment → stage?

explanations_tiered.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Improper syntelic attachment means the cell is still in prometaphase, not true metaphase.

publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Both sister kinetochores attached to same pole, preventing anaphase.
publishing_metadata.revision_note: Alignment alone ≠ metaphase; must have bipolar attachment.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Introduce spindle checkpoint as quality control.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Tell how mitosis produces daughter cells as an exact copy

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: During anaphase I, homologous chromosomes separate but sister chromatids remain attached. What would happen if sister chromatids separated instead of homologs in anaphase I?

options.A: The resulting cells would be diploid after meiosis I
options.B: Crossing over would be prevented in prophase I
options.C: The cells would have half the DNA content but normal ploidy

questions.hint: Think about the ploidy outcome if sisters separate early.
questions.explanation: If sister chromatids separated in anaphase I, each daughter would still receive one of each homolog (since homologs would still be together), maintaining diploidy. Option D would occur if this persisted through both divisions.

source_trace.content_anchor: The centromere joining sister chromatids do not separate

explanations_tiered.definition: Anaphase I normally separates homologous chromosomes, not sister chromatids, to reduce ploidy.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: If sister chromatids separated prematurely, each cell would still receive both homologs, remaining diploid.
explanations_tiered.beginner: If you split the two copies of the same shoe (sisters) instead of separating left and right shoes (homologs), each cell still has one left and one right — still diploid.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Sister separation in anaphase I would mean each daughter cell still contains both homologous chromosomes (one from each parent), so ploidy remains 2n.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Proper anaphase I requires cohesin cleavage along chromosome arms but not at centromeres; premature centromeric cohesin cleavage would cause equational division in meiosis I, mimicking mitosis.
reasoning_steps: Recall that homolog separation reduces ploidy.
Sister separation without homolog separation leaves both homologs in each cell.
Therefore, ploidy remains 2n.
misconceptions: Some think any separation reduces ploidy; not true — must separate homologs.
Sister separation in meiosis I leads to diploid cells after meiosis I.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Anaphase I error: sisters separate → cells remain diploid.

glossary_entry.definition: Meiosis I stage where homologous chromosomes separate; sister chromatids remain attached.

flashcard.front: What if sister chromatids separate instead of homologs in anaphase I?
flashcard.back: Cells would remain diploid after meiosis I.

explanations_tiered.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Separating sisters instead of homologs leaves both homologs in each cell, so cells remain diploid.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Result of sister chromatid separation in anaphase I?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Cells would be diploid after meiosis I.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Stage where homologous chromosomes separate, reducing ploidy.
publishing_metadata.revision_note: Anaphase I error (sisters separate) → no reduction.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Contrast with normal anaphase I (homolog separation) and anaphase II (sister separation).
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Outline the pattern of cell division by meiosis

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: A cell in G2 phase has completed DNA replication but detects unrepaired DNA damage. What is the most likely outcome?

options.A: The cell will immediately enter mitosis
options.B: The cell cycle will arrest at the G2/M checkpoint until repair occurs
options.C: The cell will skip directly to cytokinesis

questions.hint: Checkpoints prevent damaged DNA from being passed on.
questions.explanation: The G2/M checkpoint detects DNA damage and halts the cycle, allowing time for repair. Option A would occur only if DNA is undamaged.

source_trace.content_anchor: cell replenishes its energy stores and synthesizes proteins necessary for chromosome manipulation

explanations_tiered.definition: G2/M checkpoint ensures DNA integrity before mitosis; damage causes cell cycle arrest.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: p53 and ATM/ATR pathways activate repair or apoptosis if damage is irreparable.
explanations_tiered.beginner: A quality control checkpoint at the end of G2 says 'stop if there's damage; fix it first'.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: DNA damage activates Chk1, which inhibits CDK1-cyclin B, preventing mitotic entry until repair is complete.
explanations_tiered.advanced: ATR/ATM activate Chk1, which phosphorylates and inhibits CDC25C, keeping CDK1-cyclin B inactive and arresting cells in G2.
reasoning_steps: Recall that checkpoints monitor for damage.
G2/M checkpoint specifically checks DNA after replication.
Damage triggers arrest until repaired.
misconceptions: Some think mitosis proceeds regardless; checkpoints prevent this.
Endoreduplication is not the immediate response to damage.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: G2 checkpoint: damage → arrest until repair.

glossary_entry.definition: Cell cycle checkpoint that prevents mitosis if DNA is damaged or replication incomplete.

flashcard.front: What happens if DNA damage is detected in G2?
flashcard.back: The cell cycle arrests at the G2/M checkpoint until repair occurs.

explanations_tiered.solution_manual_note: Answer B. The G2/M checkpoint arrests the cycle to allow DNA repair before mitosis.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: DNA damage in G2 → what happens?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Cycle arrests at G2/M checkpoint for repair.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Control point that halts cell cycle if DNA is damaged or incompletely replicated.
publishing_metadata.revision_note: G2 damage → arrest at G2/M checkpoint.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Link to cancer: loss of checkpoints leads to genomic instability.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Outline the series of events taking place in the cell cycle

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: At the end of telophase II, four cells are present. If each cell has 7 chromosomes, what was the diploid number (2n) of the original mother cell?
questions.explanation: Meiosis reduces chromosome number by half. If final cells are haploid with n=7, the original diploid number 2n = 14. Option A would mean no reduction; C would be double.

source_trace.content_anchor: four daughter cells each with half the chromosome number of the initial parent cell

explanations_tiered.definition: Meiosis halves the chromosome number, so if haploid n=7, diploid 2n=14.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Haploid gametes have n chromosomes; the original cell was 2n.
explanations_tiered.beginner: If you end with 7, and meiosis halves the number, you started with 14.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Since gametes have n=7, the original diploid zygote had 2n=14.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Reduction division in meiosis I halves ploidy; telophase II cells are haploid (n).
worked_solution: Step 1: Identify haploid number from final cells: n = 7.
Step 2: Recall original mother cell was diploid: 2n.
reasoning_steps: Note final cells are haploid (n).
Original mother cell was diploid (2n).
Multiply n by 2 to get 2n.
misconceptions: Some forget meiosis halves number, so they double instead of halving.
Confusing haploid and diploid when reversing the calculation.

glossary_entry.definition: Having one set of chromosomes (n), typical of gametes.

explanations_tiered.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Haploid n=7 means original mother cell was 2n = 14.

publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Single set of chromosomes (n), produced by meiosis.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Outline the pattern of cell division by meiosis
